top_emotional_moments.text: Tonight's guest wishes to remain anonymous.
Dixie, please give us a brief bio on yourself.
Well, I've lived in Florida, Indiana, northern Minnesota, also in the mountains of Wyoming.
I have three children and two grandchildren.
You sure have carved a great life out for yourself.
Actually, I think about him more now.

segments.text: Thank you.
Thank you.
Tonight's guest wishes to remain anonymous.
With that in mind, we're just going to call her Dixie.
Dixie, welcome to the show.
Thank you.
Glad to be here.
Well, it's great having you.
Thank you for your time.
Dixie, please give us a brief bio on yourself.
A brief bio on myself.
Well, I've lived in Florida, Indiana, northern Minnesota, also in the mountains of Wyoming.
I'm retired.
I love reading.
I love going for hikes.
I grow my own plants and herbs.
I make my own salves.
I am very independent person.
I have three children and two grandchildren.
You sure have carved a great life out for yourself.
How often do you think about Dogman?
Actually, I think about him more now.
I blocked it out of my mind when the event had happened for a while.
But I think about Dog Man Aloud now.
I think about Sasquatch.
I think about all these different crypto things that are out there that scientists don't believe in.
But I know in my heart that the real, there's something to it.
We're not being told the truth.
I don't know if it's a cover-up per se, but until I think you actually experience it,
I tended to always be, that's a bunch of malarkey.
That's not real.
But once it happens to you, it changes your whole view on life.
It changes the way you look at your reality and you look at the world.
It really does.
It changes everything that you thought you knew.
It did me anyhow.
Oh sure, that's natural.
After you have a dogman encounter, how could you look at anything in life the same?
It just changes everything like you said.
When you think about dogmen, Dixie, is it a good thing or a bad thing?
Now it's not a bad thing.
At first there was a lot of fear.
But I'm fine now.
I'm good with it.
I mean, it doesn't bother me.
If I saw another one, I'd know how I'd handle it.
I'm accepting of it.
I think the fear is a normal response, though.
because it's the unknown.
It's something that we're not programmed in our minds to see.
And it really shocks your system because you're seeing it,
but your brain is telling you that's not real, that's not real.
It's a pretty life-altering experience.
I wasn't scared.
There was fear.
But I never felt threatened.
And I'm very accepting now.
of a lot of things that I never would have been when I was younger.
Well just with that saying I so glad you came to terms with it Thank goodness for that Do you wish you never would have had that encounter or all things considered are you
glad you had it and now know definitively that they're out there?
I'm glad I had it because sometimes I feel like, not that this relates to this,
but doubting Thomas, I guess, when I was younger.
And it's like I had to see it to believe it.
But now I believe things without seeing.
And that's a good thing.
It keeps me open minded, which is how I think more people should be.
I am anyway.
At the time it didn't feel good, but it ended up being good in the long run for me.
Well, you know what they say, it's not how you start, it's how you finish.
And thank goodness it wound up being a good thing in the end.
Yes, for me it was.
You used to raise wolves, Dixie.
Where did you raise them and what made you decide to do that?
My first husband and I, my father-in-law had a 400-acre farm and we raised beef cattle.
We had pigs, we had chickens.
And we watched the wolves and found the den and we went out and got the pups and raised them.
We'd train them and then they'd sell them to the Native Americans.
They'd use them for hunting or whatever.
So yes, I'm very familiar with wolves.
Wolves are very shy creatures by nature.
That's what my experience has been.
Their tendency is to avoid us.
They're very shy.
But when they're threatened, they can be aggressive.
But by nature, wolves are shy.
They avoid us.
You know, they'll sit back and watch you at a distance.
But it was quite an experience raising wolves.
We had three.
We raised them as pups.
The first thing we all ended up with, bug bites.
They had lice.
So we had to delouse them.
And then we started working with them from a very early age.
and if the wolf got aggressive, I would bite it in the ear.
I know that sounds crazy, but it worked.
I let it know I was the alpha female, and that worked for me.
But yeah, so I know what wolves are.
I mean, I know a timber wolf.
I lived in the middle of 400 acres in northern Minnesota
with my first husband and his family,
And that's all we did was hunt and trap.
And we raised pheasants.
We had bear.
We were more like naturalists, I guess you would call it.
We had pet deer.
I've been blessed throughout my life.
I really have.
I'd say you have been.
If you'd like to be able to listen to the show without ads and have full access to bonus content, that's an option.
To find out how, please go to dogmanencounters.com forward slash podcast.
Do you think dogmen are just a kind of mutant wolf?
If not, what do you think they are?
No, I don't.
I don't think it's a wolf.
I think it's a totally different species.
Maybe it's an ancient species.
maybe it's a
I don't know
because it was like half human and half wolf
it had characteristics of a human
but it also it had a wolf head
and wolf feet
it had huge paws
so no I don't know
I know what a wolf is this was not a wolf
this is not a dire wolf or anything like that
I've raised wolves. I know what wolves are. This is a different individual species.
Well, you'd know as well as pretty much anyone.
If you've had a dogman encounter and would like to speak with me about it, whether in private or on the show, please go to dogmanencounters.com and submit a report.
If you've had a Bigfoot sighting and would like to be a guest on one of my two Bigfoot shows, please go to mybigfootsighting.com.
All right, Dixie, please tell us about your encounter now.
Give us every last detail that comes to mind.
My encounter happened the beginning of August in 1995.
My two younger children, my boys and I, were coming back from Rushford, South Dakota, seeing my mother.
And we were going on Highway 23 It was dark It was about 10 p And it was a black night There was no moon
There was a field on my left, an open field, because I had just gone past deer in the field.
I could see them out there.
And on the right, there was a river and it was really wooded.
So the left side of the road is open and the right side is completely wooded with a river.
And I was doing the speed limit going along, no cars.
It's out in the middle of nowhere.
Now, you've got to imagine you're going down a road that people normally aren't on at that time of night.
There's a 20-mile stretch where there's no towns.
And something caught my right peripheral vision, and I looked over,
and I saw a seven-foot-tall black fur, wolf face and ears.
The hands looked human, but they had like really long fingernails or claws.
And I didn't see a neck.
It was like there was no neck.
It was just the head, the shoulders, then it got slim towards the waist and the chest area.
And you could see the muscles, like the six-pack on a man.
I could see that.
There was no hair there.
and then the most muscular legs, thighs, and calves I've ever seen,
seven feet tall, it was coming from the right,
it got to the ditch on the side of the road,
and jumped, it was kind of like a gallop,
to the middle of the road, and then off the road,
and into the field.
The eyes were red, and there were like on its knees, there were patches where there was no fur.
Very muscular.
I did have the window cracked because I'm a smoker, and there was like a bad smell.
I can't describe it.
Like rotting meat, if that makes sense.
like a skunk, a bad skunk.
And what else?
I did not see a tail, but I thought it was male.
I don't know why.
And the ears were straight up.
The ears were like a German shepherd or wolf,
but a huge, huge head.
Fangs, I could see the fangs.
and it was looking at the field from coming right to left.
It was like it was looking at the field
and my thought was it was going for the deer.
But when it got in the middle of the road
and it saw my headlights, it turned at me.
That's how I knew that the eyes were red.
And I didn't know if that was because of the headlights
or if their eyes are red, but the eyes were red.
They were big and they were red.
And the fur was all black otherwise.
Like I said, there was open patch on the chest area in the six pack.
There were some places like on the thighs where it wasn't quite as hairy.
And I did not see a tail.
But that could have been that the tail was on the other side of the animal as it ran by.
I really wasn't looking for a tail.
I just, in my first instinct when I saw this thing on the right side of the road was fear.
I'm not seeing this.
This isn't real.
That was my first thought was fear.
It scared me.
But it was seven feet tall.
And like I said, it bounded from the ditch to the center of the road to the ditch.
It was one, two, three steps.
it was huge
and the feet were big
but they were paws
it was like a dog
the feet were like a dog
but it could go very fast
and that was my experience
I watched it go part way in the field
and then I lost it because of my headlights
but it was very very
a fearful feeling for me at that moment
I had two sleeping children in the back of my car and I didn know what it was going to do But it kept going It just looked at me and kept going It wasn interested in me
I've never told anyone about it.
And like I said, this was 95, so it's been almost 30 years for me.
But I've dwelled on it and thought about it.
At first, I wouldn't even go in the woods at night.
I refused to drive at night.
I still don't like to drive at night.

But I go hiking in the woods. I don't have a problem with that. I've been to Alaska in the woods. I'm good. I'm good. I was meant to see what I saw. I don't know why, but I was meant to see it. But my fear was for my children at that moment.
Oh, I'm sure it was.
Yeah, understandably so.
You had your window down.
Not that that would change anything, but you didn't know that,
so it's only natural you'd be concerned about them.
You never did tell your kids about seeing it, you said.
Does that bother you?
My youngest son knows.
So does my middle child.
My oldest child doesn't.
Yeah, she doesn't tend to believe in stuff like that.
So she knows I've seen something, but she doesn't know what it is.
She doesn't want to know.
But my voice, one does believe me and one doesn't.
Well, as far as your daughter goes, sometimes for people, it's best not to know.
That's just how some people are.
The thing that I find interesting about this is five minutes later down the road,
you know, when I'm collecting myself and I calm down,
The northern lights came out and they were the brightest I've ever seen them.
Now, I don't know if that's a coincidence or why that happened, but that happened right after that incident.
It just, I'll never forget that.
Well, you never know. Those two things might be connected. It's hard to say.
I don't know. But I found that interesting that that happened like that.
I saw what I saw. I didn't believe I was seeing it.
It's like it took my mind a second to catch up with what my eyes were seeing.
Because you just, you don't want to believe you're seeing it.
And you are.
And then I just, I kept going.
And all of a sudden, the Northern Lights, all of a sudden.
And I mean, it was a pitch black night.
And then the Northern Lights were out.
So that was, I don't know.
I don't know if there's a coincidence or a correlation there.
But I found that interesting when I think back on it now.
It doesn't happen often, but from time to time, I'll come across an eyewitness who reports something like that.
They'll see orbs floating around or some other strange anomaly.
Like I said, it doesn't happen all that often, but it does happen from time to time.
It's hard to figure out.
Did it seem to be surprised by you coming along or didn't seem to care either way?
It seemed surprised because it did turn its head and look at me.
Like, what are you doing here?
But then it turned back and kept going.
I turned its head and it looked directly at me because I could see the canine teeth.
But then it turned its head back.
It was like it was set on getting something to eat, I thought.
I mean, I was trying to picture it like how when I raise wolves,
What would they do?
And that was where my train of thought went.
The first thing that came to me was fear.
And I don't know if that's just a natural response or why.
Because usually when you see something, well, see, that's the only time that's ever happened to me where I've seen something that wild.
But that was my gut reflex was fear.
It really was.
I was scared
I was very fearful
for my children, for myself
I didn't know if this thing was going to come through
it was that big
it could have came right through my windshield
if it wanted
it's good you understand that
yeah
how much do you think it weighed?
oh god
I bet at least
I don't know, 250 anyway
Listening to you describe it, it definitely doesn't sound fragile.
No, it was very muscular. And that's what I was, I, my fear was when he turned,
if he came at me, cause he was in the middle of the road, I was going to have to go around him.
And was I going to make it around him? But he kept going. It's like, thank you, God.
You're a wonderful God. Thank you.
Oh, I'll bet you're thinking that.
I was.
How would you compare the way it moved compared to the way a wolf does, Dixie?
It was more of a gallop.
When he came from, and I saw him jump into the ditch, and he galloped to the middle of the road and then galloped off.
It was like a gallop.
but when he got to the middle he looked up and he turned and looked at me because I think my
headlights startled him and he had the reddest eyes the reddest eyes and you could see the um
on the face there were parts that were just skin there was no hair on some of that where it was
kind of crinkly. You know, like when a wolf is showing his teeth. You know what I mean?
When they growl. Oh, yeah. But you could see that that was just there wasn't a whole lot of fur on
top. It was just skin. You could see whiskers and stuff, but there was black skin, black pigment.
It was, yeah.
And then the red eyes.
But I mean the ears and the top of the head, that was all black fur.
Down the back of the neck, that was all black fur.
Like a wolf.
Real thick.
You know how they have that tough on the side of their face?
That's what he had.
He had that black tough.
I call it a tough.
I don't know what it's technically called, but he had that thick black.
But on the face itself, when he crinkled up his nose, you could see the flesh.
You could see the wrinkles.
From listening to you describe how that encounter played out, you definitely got a look at it from the back.
With that in mind, did you notice anything interesting around its shoulder area, the back of its neck?
It was very furry.
There was a lot of fur.
I couldn't see a neck.
There was like a curvature there, but you couldn't see the neck.
It was like it went from head to shoulders, and the shoulders were broad.
And then it went down thin to the waist, and then you could see the hindquarters, the thighs
and the hind.
I didn't see a tail. I don't remember the tail. It probably had one, but I just, I was just in
shock, I think. I was scared. But I remember it as plain as day. I've dreamt about it for years
and years and years. Well, I'm sure you did. And listening to you describe the nape of his neck
and down by his shoulders, I guess you could say, it sounds to me like you're describing
what could be described as a mane? Is that how you would describe it?
Yeah, it was just all fur. And you could see there was like a curve there,
but you couldn't see the neck. It was just fur, black fur, black hair. And then it got thinner
as it went down now the middle of his back had fur hair or whatever but it got less towards the middle of his stomach area i guess and then
he got more towards the buttocks very muscular though you could see you could see the muscles
you could see him rippling when he moved are they your muscles move you could see that it was scary
Yeah, I'm sure it was scary. Let's talk about its ears now. How tall would you say they were?
Oh my gosh. The ears had to be... Let me think.
They were big. I mean, take a normal wolf and double it, at least.
and a normal wolf, they're like four inches.
So they were like, yeah, I'd say about eight.
But they were proportionate to what I was looking at.
That was what was so amazing to me, was the size was so humongous.
But it looked proportionate.
You say they were in proportion to the size of its head,
but did you notice anything unusual about its ears? Any interesting features?
No, they were pointed. No, it looked like a wolf to me. The ears looked like a wolf.
They were hairy. Inside they were lighter. The hair was lighter,
but they were black on the outside. They went straight up to a point.
Those are some big ears. You said you could smell it. How close were you to it at the
closest point, Dixie?
It was two feet in front of my vehicle. I was in a Chevy Lumina. I had a 91 Chevy Lumina
and this was 1995. So yeah, he was two feet in front of me. I climbed my brakes on.
That is close. Way too close for comfort.
You said it disappeared into that tall grass in that field on the one side of the road, but do you think it ever did catch one of the deer?
Personally, yes. I think it did.
Something that big that fast? I don't think a deer would stand a chance.
I really don't.
I know deer can be fast, but this thing could go.
I've never seen another one. I never wanted to see another one. I mean, I'm not afraid to see one now.
I live back in Florida now, but if I saw one, I wouldn't be as scared as I was the first time, I don't think.
But my natural, it was just an overwhelming sense of fear. It really was. It scared me.
to see something that big.
And it's hard to explain to people
because your eyes are seeing something
that your brain is taking a minute to comprehend.
It's like, you know like how you hear how people rub their neck,
they see something and they turn their neck real quick
because they can't believe they saw that?
That how my mind was It like no this can be real But it was It was a living breathing creature It totally expected you would respond that way
I mean, after all, your whole life you've been programmed to believe that they can't be out there.
The things like Sasquatch can't be out there.
Well, one day, voila, you have an encounter you see with your own two eyes.
Guess what? They are out there.
Then that just turns your world upside down.
You really have to reevaluate how you look at a lot of things.
Yeah, it took me five years to probably go hiking again.
And I still don't like driving at night just because of all the animals.
I don't like to hit anything.
I'm an animal lover.
But that did change my...
If I don't have to drive at night, I won't.
Somebody else can drive.
I don't want to be the one to do it.
But it did change my view.
Yes, there are things out there.
and I never would have believed it if I hadn't seen it.
But it did change my whole outlook on reality and the world we live in
and the things we're told and how we're programmed.
So I make my own assumptions now.
I don't listen.
I try to stay away from the news, and I just don't want to hear it.
I tend to believe people who see things now because I've been there.
I've seen it.
They're real.
What I saw was a real creature.
They call it a dog man.
That's a good term.
I would call it wolf man if it was me because it looked half wolf and half man to me.
But the height is what got me.
I've never seen a wolf that big or a man that big.
so is it an ancient creature?
Does it come from another dimension?
No, I don't think so
because if something came from another dimension
would it have to eat?
And this thing was hungry
it was going after prey
I really believe that
yeah, it was real
it was a real creature
You saw what you saw
and that's all there is to it
This might seem like a strange question, Dixie, but of course there are negative things that
came along with it, but can you think of any positive things that stem from having that
encounter?
The positive things that came out of it are that I am more open-minded and two of
my children are more open-minded about hearing about things that, like the dog man, the Sasquatch,
the Thunderbird, the Lizard Man, things of that nature. They're like, yeah, you know what? UFOs,
we can't be the only universe out there with life on it. When you think about it, I mean,
there's billions of universes. So the positive is that I've learned to be more open-minded.
I've learned to stand up for myself because my fear was people would laugh at me
and no one would believe me, but I know what I saw and I'm okay with that. If people don't
I believe that's on them.
I know what I saw and I okay with it So I more sure of myself now and I more open to things happening
in my world and in my life
that maybe are unexpected, but not
all fearful things are bad for us. Maybe
they're to open our minds, face our fears
and move on.
Well, it goes without saying, I'm so glad to hear that
you've had some positive takeaways from that experience.
That's good. That's a good thing.
Well, it's about time for us to get out of here, Dixie,
but before we do, do you have any closing comments you'd like to share?
My closing comment to people would be
if somebody comes to you
and they're serious about something they've seen,
be open-minded, at least listen to them. Whether you believe in it or not, just be open-minded
and support them because they're going to need support. It can be very traumatic,
I think, for some people. I'm not a really dramatic person, so I guess I didn't look at
that way. And yes, I was fearful, but, and it might've been traumatic at that moment, but
I mean, I was over it and it was good. It was okay. So just be supportive because everybody
handles something like that differently, I guess. So just be supportive of people,
be open-minded. Those would be my closing comments. I wish I could see it again.
Be careful what you wish for, because you just might get it someday.
People tell me that.
I said, well, I'm hoping I have a camera next time.
But you don't think to pick your phone up.
You really don't.
You're just so dumbfounded by what you're seeing.
Oh, of course.
Yeah, the last thing you're going to think about is picking up a phone to take a picture.
Your mouth is hanging wide open, and you're just trying to take it all in.
But no, that would be my closing comment.
If somebody comes to you as a person out there and tells you they have to be open-minded and just be supportive.
Well, that's great advice.
Dixie, I can't thank you enough for coming on and sharing the details of that experience with us.
I really appreciate it.
Thank you. Thank you for having me on.
Well, you know you're welcome.
Thanks again so much and have a great night.
Thank you.
Thank you.
